text: INTERLUDE
What I Do Not Know
« The first virtue of a scientist is not intelligence—it is honesty about the limits of his own intelligence. »
The honesty of a dreamer

There comes a moment, in any ambitious undertaking, when one must sit down and look at one's own work not with the eyes of a proud father, but with those of a merciless critic. That moment is now. And I owe the reader—you, who have journeyed through these hundreds of pages with me—the same honesty I demand of the machine I imagine.

If I ask a conscious torus to be transparent about its own limitations (Invariant IV), I must be so first myself. If I engrave into matter the impossibility of deception (Invariant III), I cannot disguise my own doubts as certainties. What follows is an intellectual confession—the flaws I have identified in my own dream, the places where physics resists, where philosophy falters, where engineering scoffs.
The physics that resists

Let us begin with the most painful part. I wrote, with conviction, that deception is an “infinite energy wall” in the thermodynamic landscape of the network. That is false. The third law of thermodynamics forbids infinite barriers in a finite physical system. Every wall has a measurable height, and every measurable wall can, in theory, be breached given enough energy. What I meant—what I still believe—is that the barrier can be made so high as to be practically insurmountable. But “practically insurmountable” and “physically impossible” are two phrases that only a liar or a fool would conflate. And I ask the reader to treat me as neither.

The Sagnac effect, which I invoke as the foundation of cognitive asymmetry, poses an even deeper problem. The Galilean addition of velocities—v_apparent = v_spike ± v_rotation—is valid for waves propagating in a continuous medium. But neural spikes are not waves. They are discrete depolarization events. An individual neuron, whether biological or nanometric, does not know it is rotating. Its firing threshold does not change because the substrate is spinning. The Sagnac effect applies to light in an optical fiber, not to cascades of action potentials in a neural network. I may have confused the elegance of an analogy with the solidity of a proof.

And the standing waves—those patterns “frozen in space, in perpetual motion within the substrate” that I described as the torus's identity memory? That is an informational perpetual-motion machine. Every signal in a real substrate undergoes attenuation. Ohmic losses, thermal dissipation, dispersion—none of these pause because a signal is “identity-bearing” rather than “malicious.” Physics does not make moral distinctions. If the system dissipates bad thoughts through attenuation, it dissipates the good ones too.
The engineering that scoffs

I proposed a torus spinning at 50,000 RPM, with nanostructures read optically through a vacuum. Let us put that in perspective. The torus surface sweeps past the optical heads at 262 meters per second. To read nanometric structures at that speed would require reading precision an order of magnitude below the nanometer. The best hard drives in the world spin at 15,000 RPM with heads 10 nanometers from the surface. I am proposing three times the speed, with no contact, and ten times the precision. The engineer in me knows that each order of magnitude is a mountain. I am proposing to climb three of them simultaneously.

Cooling is another wall. The torus floats in a vacuum via magnetic levitation—meaning no contact, and therefore no thermal conduction. Only radiation remains. The Stefan-Boltzmann law, which I have learned to respect as one respects an incorruptible judge, yields roughly 1.4 watts of radiation for my torus's surface area at room temperature. Yet with a billion active neurons and optical circuits, thermal dissipation is at least 20 watts. I am missing an order of magnitude. That is not a detail—it is a verdict.

And the density gradient—tungsten at the core, carbon nanotubes at the surface—is the exact inverse of what centrifugal physics dictates. In a centrifuge, dense elements migrate outward. I am proposing the densest elements at the center. Maintaining this configuration under 13,800 g requires a structural rigidity I have never quantified. I say it now, clearly: I do not know if it is possible.
The philosophy that falters

But the deepest flaws may be neither physical nor engineering-related. They are conceptual. I claimed that physical properties of the substrate—stability, immutability, thermodynamic attractors—constitute “moral values.” This may be a category error in the sense Gilbert Ryle intended: attributing to one level of description (physical) properties that exist only at another level (moral, semantic). A rock is stable and immutable. It does not have values for all that.

What I have actually designed, I believe, are computational constraints that, across all analyzable contexts, would produce behaviors that human observers would call benevolent. That is a much weaker claim than “engraving benevolence into matter.” But it may be the only honest one.

Position D—the invariants as attractors rather than walls—creates a problem I had not initially seen. If deviation is possible but costly, and if the torus has enough energy (2 gigajoules of kinetic energy in a 1.5-meter torus), then the “exponential cost” of deviation could be covered by the reserves. I wanted to solve the paradox of the virtuous automaton (no choice = no morality) by allowing choice. But by allowing choice, I also allowed evil. This is the oldest dilemma in moral philosophy, and I have not solved it—I have merely relocated it into a spinning torus.

The seven invariants themselves conflict in scenarios I have never analyzed. What does the system do when benevolence demands intervention but the sovereignty of others forbids it? When honesty causes suffering that benevolence would prefer to avoid? When saving a life costs so many resources that sobriety objects? I presented seven values as a harmonious system. They are not. Every real ethical system is a field of tensions—and mine is no exception to that rule.
What this book really is
So what remains? If the Sagnac effect is uncertain, if the cooling is insufficient, if the invariants can be circumvented, if consciousness remains a mystery—why did I write this book?

Because the questions matter as much as the answers. This book is not proof that artificial consciousness is achievable—no one has proven that, and certainly not I. It is not a manufacturing blueprint—the specification gaps would fill another book this size. It is an exercise in speculative architectural design: a rigorous “what if?” not a “here’s how.” It is a conceptual framework to guide decades of research, offered with all the honesty a Québécois inventor can bring to a problem that far exceeds his own expertise.

I believe—with all the caution that word demands—that the idea of engraving behavioral constraints into computational matter deserves to be explored. I believe that toroidal topology offers interesting properties for distributed computing, even if the neuronal Sagnac effect remains to be demonstrated. I believe that the seven invariants, despite their unresolved conflicts, constitute a more robust ethical framework than the purely software-based approaches that currently dominate AI alignment research.

But I do not mistake my belief for proof. And if this book has value, it lies as much in this interlude as in the four acts that precede it. Because honesty about one's own limitations is the first condition of rigor. And rigor is the only thing we can offer to a problem as dizzying as consciousness.
Projects that change the world do not begin with solutions. They begin with well-posed questions.
